not_claimed: This does not prove a new mathematical theory.
This is not a claim about current clinical practice.
This does not prove empirical value of the entire framework.
This is not the same as the constructed non-factorization proof with two artificial worlds.
proved_locally: In this public kidney stone dataset, aggregate success rate recommends Treatment B, while stone-size-conditioned success rates recommend Treatment A for both small and large stones.
pi: aggregate treatment success rate ignoring stone size
D: stone-size-conditioned treatment choice by highest observed success rate
operational_conclusion: Aggregate treatment success rate is the wrong object for a stone-size-conditioned treatment decision.
source_status: public historical clinical dataset used as a canonical Simpson's paradox example

primary_study.label: Charig C.R., Webb D.R., Payne S.R., Wickham J.E. Comparison of treatment of renal calculi by open surgery, percutaneous nephrolithotomy, and extracorporeal shockwave lithotripsy. British Medical Journal. 1986;292:879-882.
primary_study.note: Original clinical study from which the kidney stone Simpson's paradox data are commonly derived.

bmj_confounding.label: Julious S.A., Mullee M.A. Confounding and Simpson's paradox. BMJ. 1994;309:1480-1481.
bmj_confounding.note: Reports and discusses the confounding / Simpson's paradox structure of the kidney stone data.

jcm_review.label: Bonovas S., Piovani D. Simpson's Paradox in Clinical Research: A Cautionary Tale. Journal of Clinical Medicine. 2023;12(4):1633.
jcm_review.note: Peer-reviewed open-access discussion reporting the kidney stone treatment data from Charig et al.
